coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots-incorporated cathode buffer for improvement of inverted polymer solar cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells

coredata.prism:issueName: Dye Sensitized Solar Cells, Organic, Hybrid Solar Cells and New Concepts- Dye Sensitized Solar Cells, Organic, Hybrid Solar Cells and New Concepts
coredata.dc:creator: Bin Yang, Hong
Qian Dong, Yong
Wang, Xizu
Yun Khoo, Si
Liu, Bin
Ming Li, Chang
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are an emerging class of nanomaterials with unique photonic and electric properties. In this study, GQDs were prepared by a facile, inexpensive and high-yield hydrothermal method and were further used as a cathode buffer additive for inverted polymer solar cells due to a wide band gap (~3.3eV) and well-matched energy level between GQDs–cesium carbonate (GQDs–Cs2CO3) modified indium tin oxide (3.8eV) and high occupied molecular orbit of [6,6]-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (3.7eV). In comparison to inverted polymer solar cells using cesium carbonate (Cs2CO3) buffer layer, the power conversion efficiency of GQDs–Cs2CO3 based device showed 22% enhancement from 2.59% to 3.17% as a result of enhanced exciton dissociation and suppressed free charge recombination at cathode/polymer active layer interface by GQDs. This work provides a new application of GQDs in organic electronic devices.

Quantum dots
Cesium carbonate
Organic solar cells
Exciton dissociation
Charge recombination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM image (a), Raman (b), UV–vis absorption and photoemission spectra (c) of as-prepared GQDs and the corresponding Tauc plot (d).

(a) UPS spectra of ITO, Cs2CO3/ITO, GQDs/ITO, and GQDs–Cs2CO3/ITO. (b) Transmittance of Cs2CO3 and GQDs–Cs2CO3 coated ITO and UV–vis absorption spectra of P3HT:PCBM films on Cs2CO3 and GQDs–Cs2CO3 coated ITO substrates. The absorption by glass/ITO substrate was subtracted in the absorption spectra. The thickness of P3HT:PCBM layer is ∼180nm.

(a) Schematic structure of an inverted polymer solar cell with GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer layer. J–V characteristics of inverted P3HT:PCBM solar cells with different buffer layers under AM1.5 illumination (b) and in the dark (c). (d) EQE spectra of devices with GQDs, Cs2CO3 and GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer layers.

(a) Difference of EQE between inverted polymer solar cells with Cs2CO3 and GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer layers. The absorption spectra of PCBM and P3HT film are included for reference. (b) Photoemission spectra of P3HT films on Cs2CO3/ITO and GQDs–Cs2CO3/ITO substrates recorded using a 530nm excitation source.
Schematic energy band diagram of inverted P3HT/PCBM solar cells with Cs2CO3 or GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer layers.
Performance and electric parameters of P3HT:PCBM inverted solar cells with different cathode buffer layers a .

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots-incorporated cathode buffer for improvement of inverted polymer solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, 62 Nanyang Drive, Singapore 637459, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering, Nanyang Technological University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Clean Energy & Advanced Materials, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Clean Energy & Advanced Materials, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials Research and Engineering, No. 3 Research Link, 117602, Singapore

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Institute for Clean Energy & Advanced Materials, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China. Tel.: +86 23 6825 4727.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are an emerging class of nanomaterials with unique photonic and electric properties. In this study, GQDs were prepared by a facile, inexpensive and high-yield hydrothermal method and were further used as a cathode buffer additive for inverted polymer solar cells due to a wide band gap (~3.3eV) and well-matched energy level between GQDs–cesium carbonate (GQDs–Cs2CO3) modified indium tin oxide (3.8eV) and high occupied molecular orbit of [6,6]-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (3.7eV). In comparison to inverted polymer solar cells using cesium carbonate (Cs2CO3) buffer layer, the power conversion efficiency of GQDs–Cs2CO3 based device showed 22% enhancement from 2.59% to 3.17% as a result of enhanced exciton dissociation and suppressed free charge recombination at cathode/polymer active layer interface by GQDs. This work provides a new application of GQDs in organic electronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Solution processable bulk heterojunction (BHJ) polymer solar cells (PSCs) are considered as a promising alternative to conventional silicon based ones [1–3] because of their low cost of roll-to-roll production and flexible device applications [4–6]. Over the past years, tremendous efforts have been made on development of efficient and stable BHJ PSCs for higher power conversion efficiency. Small bandgap polymers with suitable band structures were synthesized to increase the visible light absorption and thus photocurrent [7,8]. New device structure and novel charge transport layer were rationally designed to enhance photo-generated charge carrier extraction from organic active layer to electrodes and increase stability of interfaces [9–12]. Among various PSCs, inverted PSCs have received growing research attention due to their superior device stability and manufacturing compatibility. In an inverted PSC, modification of indium tin oxide (ITO) with a buffer layer is needed to reduce ITO work function and enhance electron collection. The most commonly used cathode buffer in inverted BHJ PSCs is solution-processed ZnO or TiO2 [13]. Very recently, Cs2CO3 became a promising cathode buffer because of its good electron injection and facile fabrication process [14–16]. However, the poor hole blocking ability of the Cs2CO3-modified ITO cathode significantly increases the recombination loss at the cathode/polymer active layer interface and reduces the overall power conversion efficiency.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a new class of nanomaterials, which have unique photonic and electronic properties and have been widely used in biological science [17], photocatalysis [18], light-emitting [19] and photovoltaic devices [20–22]. In photovoltaics, GQDs were used as electron acceptors in organic/inorganic hybrid solar cells and as sensitizers in nanocrystalline QD-sensitized solar cells, however the power conversion efficiencies of the respective solar cells are quite low.

In this work, we investigated GQDs as a cathode buffer additive in inverted PSCs, showing that GQDs can act as an excellent cathode buffer additive in inverted PSCs to increase the power conversion efficiency from 2.57% to 3.17% due to enhanced exciton dissociation and suppressed free charge recombination at the cathode/polymer active layer interface with the introduced GQDs in the buffer layer.

GQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method developed in the literature [23]. In a typical synthesis, 2g of citric acid monohydrate (9.5mmol) and 1g of L-cysteine (8.3mmol) were dissolved in 5mL of deionized water. The solution was evaporated until dry at 70°C for 12h. The resulting viscous syrup was heated in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 200°C for 3h. Finally, the black syrup product was collected and dissolved in 2-ethoxyethanol.

All devices with the structure of ITO/cathode buffer (GQDs, Cs2CO3 or GQDs–Cs2CO3)/P3HT:PCBM/V2O5/Au were fabricated on precleaned ITO patterned glass substrates. The cathode buffer was deposited on ITO substrates by spin-coating the GQDs (1mg/ml), Cs2CO3 (1 or 2mg/ml) or GQDs (2mg/ml)–Cs2CO3 (2mg/ml) (1:1) in 2-ethoxyethanol at 4000rpm for 60s. Following, the buffer layer coated ITO substrates were baked on a hotplate at 120°C in air for 15min. Afterwards, Poly(3-hexylthiophene-2,5-diyl) (P3HT, from Aldrich, regioregularity ≥90.0%) and [6,6]-Phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM), from Nano-C (99.5% purity) blend in 1:1 weight ratio at a P3HT concentration of 20mg/ml in chlorobenzene was spin-coated on the baked ITO substrates at 700rpm for 25s (the active layer thickness was estimated to be ∼180nm), and dried in a covered Petri dish for 20min. Subsequently, the vanadium oxide (V2O5) anode buffer was deposited on top of the polymer layer by spin-coating a vanadium precursor solution,vanadium oxytriisopropoxide in isopropanol (1:100v/v), at 5000rpm. The device fabrication was completed by thermal evaporation of 50nm gold film on top of the V2O5 buffer in vacuum (P<1.5×10−5 Pa) as anode electrode. The illumination area of each device is defined by the area of gold electrode which is ∼7.06mm2. The as-prepared devices were then annealed at 150°C for 20min in vacuum to improve the crystallinity of the P3HT:PCBM blend.

The size and size distribution of GQDs was examined with atomic force microscopy (Nanoman, Veeco, Santa Barbara, CA) using tapping mode and transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM JEOL 2100). The optical properties of GQDs were studied using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu). The photoluminesce (PL) of P3HT film was recorded using an LP920-KS instrument from Edinburgh Instruments, equipped with an iCCD camera from Andor and a photomultiplier tube. The work function of ITO electrodes modified with different cathode buffers was examined using ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy with the Helium line (21.2eV).

The current density (J)–voltage (V) characteristics were recorded with a Keithley 2420 source meter in the dark and under illumination using a Sun 2000 solar simulator (Abet) with AM1.5 spectrum (100mA/cm2). The intensity of the solar simulator was determined by a standard Si photovoltaic cell calibrated by the National Renewable Energy Laboratory. External Quantum Efficiency (EQE) measurements were performed without bias illumination with respect to a calibrated silicon diode. The EQE was calculated using EQE(λ)=(hc/λ)×(I(λ)−I dark)/P(λ), where I(λ) and I dark are the short circuit current recorded under illumination and in the dark, respectively. No corrections were made for reflection from the glass substrate. The monochromic light used for EQE measurement was supplied from xenon light, which passes through a Cornerstone monochromator. A chopper was placed after the monochromator and the signal was collected by Merlin lock-in radiometry after amplification through the current preamplifier. All measurements metioned above were performed in air at room temperature without encapsulation.

Fig. 1a shows the atomic force microscopy (AFM) image of GQDs distributed on mica substrate. The as-synthesized GQDs have sizes in a range from 0.5 to 3.5nm with a low degree of aggregation, which is consistent with the TEM measurement. The chemical structural information of GQDs was examined with Raman spectroscopy. Two major features of D and G bands were observed at around 1380 and 1575cm−1 in Raman spectrum (Fig. 1b), and can be assigned to the defect-activated and sp2-bonded carbon Raman mode, respectively [24]. The relative D- and G-band intensity (I D/I G) of the as-synthesized GQDs is ~0.5, indicating high quality of grapheme [17]. UV–vis absorption and photoemission spectra of GQDs in Fig. 1c show two typical absorption peaks at ∼240 and 343nm, respectively, of which the former is attributed to the π→π⁎ transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, while the latter is due to electron excitation from the ground states to the excited-states, resulting in strong and narrow photoluminescence (Fig. 1c). Based on Tauc's relation αhν=A(hν−E g) n
[25], where α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the photon energy, E g is the optical band gap and n equals 2 or 1/2 for indirect and direct allowed transition, respectively, an estimated direct optical bandgap of the as-prepared GQDs is ∼3.3eV (Fig. 1d).

The work function of Cs2CO3, GQDs, and Cs2CO3–GQDs modified ITO surface was investigated using ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) for comparison. As shown in Fig. 2a, a work function shift of 0.55eV towards the higher energy level was observed by coating ITO surface with Cs2CO3 or GQDs. When both Cs2CO3 and GQDs are coated on ITO surface, an additional work function shift of 0.20eV towards a higher energy level was achieved. These results clearly show that the work function of ITO is able to be tuned by coating ITO surface with an inorganic buffer. The work function of ITO was decreased from 4.55eV to 3.8eV with a surface modification by solution-processed GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer. As compared with Cs2CO3 or GQD buffer alone, the hybrid GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer obviously has a better energy match with the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of PCBM (3.7eV). The well-matched energy level at electrode/polymer active layer interface of BHJ PSCs could minimize the energy loss at the interface during charge separation to result in higher open-circuit voltage (V oc) and larger short-circuit current (J sc).

In inverted PSCs a good cathode buffer should be made from a material with wide bandgap material to only absorb UV photon and thus to minimize the energy lost from light absorption in the buffer layer. The measured transmission and absorption of GQD buffer were used to investigate its impact on the optical properties of the polymer active layer. As shown in Fig. 2b, the GQDs–Cs2CO3 coated ITO glass is highly transparent with transmittance >80% in the visible region, while the slightly lower transmittance of ITO/GQDs–Cs2CO3 in 350–450nm is due to the absorption by GQDs. The insertion of GQD buffer did not affect too much of the light absorption in the polymer active layer.

The inverted-type of BHJ PSCs were fabricated using a spin-coating method. Fig. 3a shows the cell design with a device structure of ITO/cathode buffer (GQDs, Cs2CO3 or GQDs–Cs2CO3)/P3HT:PCBM/V2O5/Au. The current–voltage characteristics under AM1.5 illumination (100mW/cm2) and in the dark of cells with different cathode buffers are displayed in Fig. 3b and c. The power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the device with GQD buffer is ∼0.79%, which is lower than the device with Cs2CO3 or GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer. However, the device with the hybrid GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer has the highest PCE with J sc=9.04mA/cm2, V oc=0.585V and FF=60.0%. Compared to the device with Cs2CO3 buffer, the overall power conversion efficiency of the device with GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer is increased by as much as 22% from 2.59% to 3.17%. One can see that the performance enhancement comes from all aspects of photovoltaic improvements in J sc, V oc and FF ( Table 1). To understand mechanism for the improvements, we measured the current–voltage characteristics of cells in dark (Fig. 3c), showing a typical diode characteristic for the device with GOD buffer. The reverse leakage current at −1V is ∼0.14mA/cm2. The estimated shunt resistance is ∼4.4×103 Ω/cm2, which is comparable to the shunt resistance of conventional BHJ PSCs, indicating a good hole blocking behavior of GQDs. Nevertheless, the large series resistance of GQD buffer (∼20Ω/cm2) limits the charge collection and thus the power conversion efficiency of the cell. When GQDs were coupled together with Cs2CO3 as the cathode buffer, the device shows a very good diode behavior with a rectification ratio of 5600 at ±1V. The introduction of GQDs in the cathode buffer not only increases the device shunt resistance, but also improves the device charge transfer by reducing the series resistance due to well matched energy level between GQDs–Cs2CO3 covered ITO and PCBM, thus improving both J
sc and V
oc . The enhanced FF of the device with GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer can also be attributed to the reduced charge recombination and enhanced charge transfer at the interface between GQDs–Cs2CO3 coated ITO and P3HT:PCBM active layer [26,27].

Fig. 3d shows the external quantum efficiency (EQE) of devices with different buffer layers. The EQE of the device with GQD, Cs2CO3 and GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer is ∼30%, 50% and 60%, respectively, at the peak of P3HT absorption, which well matches with the corresponding J sc. However, a difference in the shape of EQE spectrum of the device with Cs2CO3 and GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer is noted, where the EQE enhancement at ∼550nm is larger than that in the region of 350–450nm. Fig. 4a clearly shows this difference by subtracting the EQE of the device with Cs2CO3 buffer from that of the device with GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer. The shape of subtracted EQE spectrum resembles quite well with the absorption spectrum of P3HT film. Considering nearly the same absorption spectra of P3HT:PCBM film on Cs2CO3 and GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer layers (as shown in Fig. 2b), this result shows that the increase in photocurrent in inverted P3HT:PCBM solar cells with the GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer is mainly due to the enhanced exciton dissociation at the P3HT/buffer interface. This conclusion could be further corroborated by comparing the steady-state photoluminescence (PL) of P3HT films on Cs2CO3/ITO and GQDs–Cs2CO3/ITO substrates. As shown in Fig. 4b, a substantial reduction in PL intensity (∼70%) was observed when P3HT film on GQDs–Cs2CO3/ITO substrate was excited with a 530nm laser, which confirms that exciton dissociation can also take place at the P3HT/GQDs–Cs2CO3 interface. This additional exciton dissociation sites (besides the P3HT/PCBM interface) in inverted P3HT/PCBM solar cells can facilitate charge separation and thus enhance the device performance ( Fig. 5).

In summary, GQDs were used as an effective cathode buffer additive to improve the performance of inverted BHJ PSCs. The GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer exhibits excellent hole blocking and electron transfer capability in inverted BHJ PSCs, which is due to the wide bandgap of GQDs and well-matched energy level between GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer and PCBM. The incorporation of GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer in inverted BHJ PSCs can not only reduce the charge recombination, but also improve the charge transfer at the cathode/polymer active layer interface. Moreover, additional exciton dissociation sites (P3HT/GQDs–Cs2CO3 interface) could be created through the introduced GQDs. Thus the overall power conversion efficiency of the inverted polymer solar cells increases by 22% from 2.57 to 3.17%. Our results suggest that GQDs–Cs2CO3 buffer fabricated by a facile solution process is compatible with roll-to-roll production, can be an excellent candidate as a cathode buffer for highly efficient inverted BHJ PSCs. This work provides a new application for GQDs in organic electronic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for financial support from the Agency of Science, Technology and Research (A⁎Star), Singapore under SERC Grant no. 122 020 3053, and the Institute for Clean Energy and Advanced Materials, Southwest University.
